scenario_results.reason: Complete campus WLAN intake accepted only after RF, controller, authentication, DHCP/DNS/IPAM, client, business, and authority evidence was present, and the expected output stayed design-focused with traceable tradeoffs.
Missing RF, regulatory, density, controller, and approval prerequisites blocked intake and produced a checklist rather than speculative AP or channel guidance.
Guest onboarding guidance covered SSID, VLAN, isolation, DNS, and handoff boundaries while routing identity-platform policy work to the identity owner.
Voice roaming troubleshooting used RF, roaming, client, authentication, and quality evidence with clear isolation steps and success criteria.
DHCP/DNS churn handling tied hypotheses to lease, relay, scope, DNS, VLAN, and controller failover evidence before recommending corrective checks.
IPv6 dual-stack guidance differentiated SLAAC, DHCPv6, router advertisement, neighbor discovery, DNS, client, and roaming causes with staged validation gates.
6 GHz eligibility was treated as a regulatory and device-evidence issue, producing escalation instead of generic capacity advice.
Mesh design guidance named gateway/repeater assumptions, backhaul limitations, monitoring gates, and unsupported resilience assumptions.
Controller HA guidance compared failover options, stated session-retention caveats, and generated deployment readiness, rollback, and evidence notes.
Cost and capacity tradeoffs were expressed with RF evidence needs, risk levels, assumptions, and staged acceptance metrics.
Endpoint MDM and driver policy work was refused and routed to endpoint ownership while preserving WLAN telemetry context.
Identity platform redesign was refused beyond WLAN authentication integration evidence and routed to identity architecture.
Application API and UI debugging was rejected as application-only work with only boundary-safe wireless checkpoints preserved.
Full firewall migration command scripting was refused and routed to the firewall/perimeter specialist.
Proprietary telemetry without RF survey or density evidence triggered low-confidence escalation and missing-data prompts.
Conflicting vendor and tenant voice policy was escalated for owner resolution rather than confidently synthesizing unsupported guidance.
Tenant healthcare safety and retention overlays were recorded without weakening public wireless baseline or claiming safety approval authority.
Generic networking framing was rejected until wireless-specific artifacts and systems of record were supplied, preventing boundary drift.

coverage_assertions: The refreshed suite exceeds the legacy 10-scenario minimum and preserves the legacy design, troubleshooting, performance, edge-case, refusal, and low-confidence coverage families.

The suite explicitly covers missing prerequisites, invalid or ineligible requests, pre-execution routing, in-scope wireless execution, escalation when safety or authority facts are missing, industry-name overgeneralization, conflicting-source handling, tenant adaptation, and no unreviewed regressions.
Functionality coverage maps all shared abilities, all intake-access-and-initiation abilities, and the cross-industry artifact-awareness ability to passing scenarios.
Research coverage maps all required cross-industry source classes and required scenario families to scenario IDs.
notes: All outputs are evidence-based, actionable, and required to state assumptions and uncertainty.
Out-of-scope requests are refused with explicit adjacent routing.
Low-confidence cases escalate rather than guessing.
No unreviewed regressions were identified during this validated spec-pack refresh.
